metadata.summary: Closed catalog of eight structure-preserving transforms applied one at a time with green tests: extract, replace conditional, introduce parameter, magic numbers, decompose, rename, move method.

content.title: Refactoring Patterns
content.summary: A closed catalog of eight structure-preserving transforms — extract method/class, replace conditional with polymorphism, introduce parameter object, replace magic numbers, decompose conditional, rename for clarity, move method — applied one at a time behind a green test suite. Each refactor is a single commit. External behavior must remain identical after every step.

why.p: Behavior-preserving transforms in small steps make code easier to understand and extend without introducing regressions. Martin Fowler's research and broad industry practice confirm that incremental, test-guarded refactoring lowers bug rates and reduces feature-delivery friction. Mixing refactoring with feature work in the same commit removes the ability to bisect regressions.

list.item: Code works but is hard to understand or touch without fear.
Adding a feature would require changes in many unrelated files.
Duplicate logic exists across the codebase.
A class or function has grown beyond a single clear responsibility.
Before adding new functionality ("make the change easy, then make the easy change").
Spreading a rename or API change across many call sites in a monorepo.
No test coverage on the target code — refactoring without a safety net silently changes behavior.
Cross-cutting architectural rewrite (monolith → services, CQRS introduction) — use software-architect patterns instead.
Refactors require domain knowledge the agent lacks (regulatory rules, financial rounding, license-encoded behavior).
During incidents or release-freeze windows — risk outweighs benefit.
Code generated by another agent in the same session — re-editing for taste, not improvement.

section.name: extract-method
section.p: Split a function longer than ~20 lines or with multiple distinct responsibilities into named sub-functions, one responsibility each.

rule.statement: Split a function longer than ~20 lines or with multiple distinct responsibilities into named sub-functions, one responsibility each.
rule.rationale: Long functions hide intent. Named sub-functions read as a table of contents and are individually testable.

example.note: process_order delegates to validate_order, calculate_totals, process_payment, send_confirmation — each under 15 lines.

section.name: extract-class
section.p: When a class has a cohesive group of fields and methods that form a natural concept, move them into a dedicated class.

rule.statement: When a class has a cohesive group of fields and methods that form a natural concept, move them into a dedicated class.
rule.rationale: Reduces coupling; the extracted class becomes independently testable and reusable.

example.note: Address extracted from User; User.address delegates to Address.get_full_address.

section.name: replace-conditional-with-polymorphism
section.p: Replace a type-switch or if/elif chain with 4+ branches with a strategy or subclass hierarchy.

rule.statement: Replace a type-switch or if/elif chain with 4+ branches with a strategy or subclass hierarchy; apply only when branches are expected to grow.
rule.rationale: Removes the need to touch a central dispatch every time a new variant is added (Open/Closed Principle).

rule.statement: Require at least 4 distinct branches before applying; 2–3 branches produce over-abstraction with more classes than the original saved.
rule.rationale: Pattern over-application — 3-branch conditional yielding a 4-class hierarchy — creates abstraction debt greater than the original.

section.name: introduce-parameter-object
section.p: When a function has 4+ parameters that travel together, group them into a typed dataclass or value object.

rule.statement: When a function has 4+ parameters that travel together, group them into a typed dataclass or value object.
rule.rationale: Named fields make call sites self-documenting and prevent positional argument errors.

section.name: replace-magic-numbers-and-strings
section.p: Replace inline literals used as business constants with named constants or enums at the module level.

rule.statement: Replace inline literals used as business constants with named constants or enums at the module level.
rule.rationale: Readers cannot infer intent from bare numbers; named constants make intent explicit and centralize change.

rule.statement: Confine replacement to in-file constants; do not promote literals to config without a clear deploy requirement.
rule.rationale: Agents tend to inline domain meaning into config that should stay in code, adding unnecessary config surface.

section.name: decompose-conditional
section.p: Extract each branch and its condition into a named predicate and named handler function to eliminate nested if/elif trees.

rule.statement: Extract each branch and its condition into a named predicate and named handler function to eliminate nested if/elif trees.
rule.rationale: Named predicates read as prose; readers understand intent without tracing logic.

section.name: rename-for-clarity
section.p: Rename any identifier whose name does not immediately convey its purpose; prefer full words over abbreviations except universally accepted ones (i, n, err).

rule.statement: Rename any identifier whose name does not immediately convey its purpose; prefer full words over abbreviations except universally accepted ones (i, n, err).
rule.rationale: Code is read far more often than written; a name that requires tracing to understand wastes every future reader's time.

antipattern.description: Public-API rename without a deprecation alias: renaming an exported symbol in the same commit as the feature work.
antipattern.reason: Breaks downstream callers; needs a deprecation shim for one release cycle and a CI rule blocking re-export removal.

section.name: move-method
section.p: Move a method to the class whose data it uses most; the method currently lives in a class it envy-accesses.

rule.statement: Move a method to the class whose data it uses most; the method currently lives in a class it envy-accesses.
rule.rationale: Feature envy is a smell that misaligns responsibility; placing the method with its data reduces coupling.

section.name: safe-refactoring-workflow
section.p: The one-pattern-per-commit loop, quality gates, and agent-specific gotchas.

rule.statement: Apply exactly one pattern per commit: plan → apply diff → run full test suite → commit if green, revert if red.
rule.rationale: Atomic commits mean a single git-bisect step can locate any regression introduced by refactoring.

rule.statement: Require at least 80% line coverage on touched code before starting; if coverage is below that threshold, write characterization tests first.
rule.rationale: Without a safety net, agents silently change behavior while claiming preservation.

rule.statement: Never mix refactoring commits with feature changes; a commit either changes behavior or changes structure, never both.
rule.rationale: Mixed commits defeat bisect and make code review impossible for reviewers trying to verify behavior preservation.

rule.statement: Demand verbatim test coverage on every touched line before claiming behavior equivalence.
rule.rationale: Agents freely convert Decimal to float, reorder short-circuit evaluation, and change exception types while calling the result equivalent.

rule.statement: Lock the agent to the closed catalog; reject any prompt that says "improve" without naming a specific pattern.
rule.rationale: Open-ended "improve" prompts produce taste-driven churn, not structured refactoring.

rule.statement: Constrain commit messages to 50-char title + at most 3-line body.
rule.rationale: Agents write verbose commit essays; clean history requires a hard constraint.

antipattern.description: Big-bang refactoring: changing many patterns in one commit.
antipattern.reason: Impossible to bisect; code review becomes meaningless.
Agents claim behavior preservation while introducing subtle off-by-ones (rounding, datetime, encoding).

antipattern.description: Over-abstraction: introducing a Strategy class for a 3-branch conditional.
antipattern.reason: Abstraction debt exceeds the original debt; force a 4-branch minimum rule in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Type-hint pollution: agent adds Optional[str] where None was already a contract violation.
antipattern.reason: Masking real bugs; require all new type hints be backed by tests that verify the None case.

antipattern.description: Cross-repo callers: monorepo-wide renames miss tests in sibling packages and dynamically-imported strings.
antipattern.reason: Use Sourcegraph batch changes or git grep validator.

antipattern.description: Decimal vs float in Python: agent freely converts between them; mixing yields TypeErrors at runtime.
antipattern.reason: Distinguish semantically (currency vs measurement); constrain the prompt to never convert without explicit type coercion.

antipattern.description: Snapshot tests: refactors break snapshot tests for cosmetic reasons (output ordering, whitespace).
antipattern.reason: Distinguish behavior-preserving snapshot updates from regressions.

section.name: subagent-architecture
section.p: Drive refactoring as a TDD-style loop with specialized subagents.

list.item: coverage-checker: reports per-line coverage on the target file/range; gates the loop entry.
refactor-planner (Sonnet/Opus): reads code, picks ONE pattern from the catalog, explains why, generates unified diff + test command.
refactor-applier: applies the diff via Edit tool, runs tests, commits if green, reverts and reports if red.
rename-propagator: handles cross-file renames via AST tools; non-LLM where possible.
simplify (built-in skill): periodic "step back, simplify what we just wrote" pass to catch over-abstraction.

template.purpose: Bash loop implementing the plan-apply-test-commit cycle.
Agent prompt: pick ONE catalog pattern, justify, output unified diff + test command.
